For longer comments the [triple-quotes](https://docs.python.org/3/tutorial/introduction.html#strings) are convenient.

> String literals can span multiple lines. One way is using triple-quotes: """...""" or '''...'''

Example:

```python
raise RuntimeError("""could not clean Solr core %s (HTTP %d)""" % (uri, response.status_code))
```

This code block bothers me for several reasons.

First, it seems overly defensive. Is it really true that sometimes metadata.long_description_content_type will be set, but other times not? Even if that's really true, it should use `vars(self.metadata).setdefault('long_description_content_type', self.long_description_content_type`.

Second, why is `long_description_content_type` being singled-out here? Why do other metadata fields not need this special handling?

Third, it seems bad form to be copying an attribute by value from one object to another. Ideally, and especially for newer attributes, there should be one place where that value is defined.

I think perhaps the bug that this PR is seeking to address is revealing a design flaw in the implementation of the attribute.

Can we make long_description_content_type work like other attributes so it doesn't require this special treatment?

@boechat107 
ah I was finally able to replicate your bug. Typically execution date is rounded to the nearest second (i.e. see [this line](https://github.com/apache/incubator-airflow/blob/master/airflow/api/common/experimental/trigger_dag.py#L35)), that's why I couldn't regenerate that bug at first when I ran a command like `airflow trigger_dag -e `2017-01-01 00:00:00.123`, because it would just round the microseconds to 0.

One thing that's worth noting is that `ValueError: unconverted data remains: ..372649` seem to suggests that the execution_date string looks like `'%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S..372649' (2 periods in the date string). Seems like a bug coming from elsewhere. 

That aside, it seems reasonable to be able to support microseconds precision, thanks for looking into this!

I did this for two reasons:

1. I edit `im_func` because, as it turns out, in Python 2, `method.__doc__` isn't writable
2. I copy the image function because all subclass reference `cls.__init__`. Thus, because of 1, all subclasses would essentially be trying to edit the parent docstring.

So instead of contend with all that I've now decided to assign trait/event docs to `cls.__doc__`.

The API documentation for POST and PUT requests says this:

> hostgroup[parent_id] 
> Optional, Nil Allowed.
> Value: number

I then tried making some POST requests, like so:

```python
>>> from nailgun import client, entities
>>> from nailgun.config import ServerConfig
>>> cfg = ServerConfig.get()
>>> path = entities.HostGroup().path()
>>> response = client.post(
...     path,
...     {'hostgroup': {'name': 'hg2', 'parent_id': 9}},
...     **cfg.get_client_kwargs()
... )
>>> response.status_code
200
>>> for field_name in ('parent', 'parent_id', 'parent_ids', 'parents'):                                   
...     field_name in response.json()
... 
False
False
False
False
>>> response.json()['ancestry']
'9'
>>> response = client.post(
...     path,
...     {'hostgroup': {'name': 'hg3', 'parent_id': response.json()['id']}},  # 18
...     **cfg.get_client_kwargs()
... )
>>> response.raise_for_status()
>>> response.json()['ancestry']
'9/18'
>>> response = client.post(
...     path,
...     {'hostgroup': {'name': 'hg4', 'parent_id': response.json()['id']}},  # 19
...     **cfg.get_client_kwargs()
... )
>>> response.raise_for_status()
>>> response.json()['ancestry']
'9/18/19'
```

As you can see, each `HostGroup` entity has a foreign key relationship to some other `HostGroup` entity. In other words, the `HostGroup` database table has a self-referencing foreign key. This foreign key is named "parent". It just so happens that when GETting a `HostGroup`, the IDs of all parents are returned as a string containing decimal IDs joined by forward slashes. (This is utterly stupid IMO. Why not return a list of integer IDs? Or, even better, just the ID of the direct parent?)

What should be done? Keep the "parent" foreign key field:

```python
'parent': entity_fields.OneToOneField(HostGroup, null=True)
```

Then, add some extra logic in to `read` that parses 'ancestry' and uses that information to populate the `parent` field.
